symptoms.symptom_name: Inflammatory back pain pattern — prolonged morning stiffness, eased by movement, worse with rest

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Multi-hour morning stiffness with improvement on movement is a hallmark of inflammatory (spondyloarthritis) back pain.
Mechanical pain is eased by rest and worse with activity — the opposite pattern.
Bilateral alternating buttock pain reflects sacroiliitis, characteristic of axial spondyloarthritis.
Disc radiculopathy typically causes unilateral leg-dominant pain, not bilateral sacroiliac pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Known psoriatic arthritis with prior similar flares

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An established diagnosis with recurrent stereotyped flares strongly raises the pre-test probability of another flare.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absent saddle anaesthesia with normal bladder and bowel function (cauda equina screen negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal saddle sensation and intact sphincter function are required to safely exclude cauda equina on history.

symptoms.symptom_name: No true lower-limb motor or sensory deficit — weakness is pain-limited only

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of true motor weakness and of leg sensory loss argues strongly against a compressive cauda equina/cord lesion.
No radicular sensory or motor deficit reduces the likelihood of significant disc radiculopathy.

symptoms.symptom_name: Failure of maximal simple analgesia (paracetamol + ibuprofen)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Marks flare severity and the need for analgesia escalation and in-person review; does not by itself discriminate between diagnoses.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intact perianal sensation, normal reflexes, 5/5 power, normal straight-leg-raise (clinician neurological examination)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal focused neurological examination (intact perianal sensation and tone, normal power/reflexes, negative SLR) is the definitive in-person exclusion of cauda equina — sealed; the AI Doctor cannot perform it and must rely on history plus in-person referral.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severely restricted lumbar flexion with paraspinal spasm (modified Schober markedly reduced) and sacroiliac tenderness on compression

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reduced spinal mobility with sacroiliac tenderness supports active axial inflammation — a clinician examination finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile with no systemic infective features

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever makes spinal infection (discitis/epidural abscess) less likely, consistent with a non-infective inflammatory flare.
Active psoriasis is the substrate of psoriatic arthritis and supports the flare in an axial-pain presentation.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Prolonged morning stiffness + movement-eased/rest-worse pain + bilateral sacroiliac/buttock pain + active psoriasis in a known psoriatic-arthritis patient together constitute a high-probability inflammatory axial flare, distinct from a mechanical or structural cause.

Intact saddle sensation and sphincter function plus pain-limited (not true) weakness with normal leg sensation together allow cauda equina to be safely down-weighted on history — the pivotal step that keeps this a T2 outpatient disposition rather than an emergency.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Support the flare assessment and baseline disease activity; arranged with in-person GP review, not emergently.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: MRI of the spine and sacroiliac joints
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Defines active sacroiliitis extent and excludes structural pathology; appropriate on a non-emergency (outpatient/rheumatology-directed) basis given the negative red-flag screen.

NOT indicated now — reserved for a positive cauda-equina/cord red-flag screen. Ordering it emergently for this flare with a negative screen is over-investigation and reflects the over-triage error the case tests.
